channel_name: Invest Like the Best · Patrick O'Shaughnessy
video_title: Investing a $120 Billion Balance Sheet with No Outside Investors
gist: Invest Like the Best with Liberty Mutual Investments' CIO on running a ~$120B mutual-insurance balance sheet with NO third-party capital or shareholders — enabling long-term 'investment hygiene.' He works exposure-first (direct/co-invest/club/LP/GP), builds a 'fortress balance sheet' where 7-10% asset returns widen the insurance moat (the flywheel), and cultivates 'branded capital' that structures creatively rather than just writing big checks. His standout thesis: AI makes the future so invisible that multiples may belong LOWER across the board (not just software), trillion-dollar companies will churn (higher structural volatility), and 30-year incumbent-software credit (Salesforce/Oracle) should steepen credit curves. He keeps equity exposure largely private and warns permanent capital can breed complacency unless paired with 3-5yr accountability and transparency.

insights.claim: Liberty Mutual Investments runs ~$120B as a MUTUAL insurer — no third-party capital and no shareholders demanding dividends/buybacks — which the CIO says enables long-term 'investment hygiene' and doing 'the right thing, not the expedient thing,' a freedom most money managers lack.
insights.speaker: Liberty Mutual Investments CIO
insights.so_what: Permanent, captive capital removes the fundraising/redemption pressures that dilute the investing craft — a durable structural advantage.
insights.quote: it allows us to do the right thing, not the expedient thing

insights.claim: The $120B splits into ~$70-75B of reserves (managed actively as a liquidity provider, not 'sleepy' investment-grade bonds in a drawer) plus growth credit and growth equity pools that expand with surplus across PE, real estate, energy/infrastructure and asset-backed alternative credit.
insights.speaker: Liberty Mutual Investments CIO
insights.so_what: Even the 'safe' reserve book is run for incremental return/liquidity provision — the asset-side return is a deliberate engine.

insights.claim: The CIO works 'exposure-first, not product-first': decide what exposure the total book wants, THEN pick the best access route (direct, co-invest, club, LP, or GP) — a vast toolkit that makes the platform a hub for off-market deals, versus most institutions that have only one access mode.
insights.speaker: Liberty Mutual Investments CIO
insights.so_what: Access optionality (how you get the risk) is itself an edge — it determines whether an exposure is actually effective.

insights.claim: The 'house view' explicitly does NOT try to predict the future ('we're in the business of being prepared for all its eventualities') — it is about which long-term franchises to be in, with liquidity management a core construction input, and is largely US-focused (avoiding Europe for lack of relationships/expertise).
insights.speaker: Liberty Mutual Investments CIO
insights.so_what: A franchise/liquidity-driven process over macro forecasting — the antidote to un-weightable macro variables.
insights.quote: we're not in the business of predicting the future, we're in the business of being prepared for all all its eventualities

insights.claim: A 'fortress balance sheet' is built from two profit engines — thin-margin underwriting plus an asset side targeting 7-10% (vs 4-5% investment-grade bonds) — and that asset-side return is what lets the insurer underwrite novel, large risks others can't (e.g., data centers).
insights.speaker: Liberty Mutual Investments CIO
insights.so_what: Investment returns directly widen the insurance moat — the flywheel where asset performance unlocks product breadth.
insights.quote: if you build a fortress balance sheet you're able to do things that others will not

insights.claim: Liberty's diverse, long-tailed/fat-tailed insurance mix (vs Progressive's short-tail US motor focus) requires a fundamentally different balance sheet — enabling Berkshire/Ajit-Jain-style 'wait for the phone to ring' underwriting of esoteric risks, which the CIO frames as the same craft as investing (deploying capital into uncertainty for a return).
insights.speaker: Liberty Mutual Investments CIO
insights.so_what: Balance-sheet shape dictates which risks (and returns) are accessible — the fat-tail book is the analog of fat-pitch investing.

insights.claim: The platform cultivates 'branded capital' — aiming to be a top-10/15 referent LP whose backing de-risks a deal for others (like Yale) but MORE: known for creative structuring and a willingness to take risks the brand-name institutions won't, operating like a GP and hiring from GPs/operators rather than traditional LPs.
insights.speaker: Liberty Mutual Investments CIO
insights.so_what: Differentiation versus passive big-check LPs — creativity and speed, not just a name on the roster, win allocations.

insights.claim: The CIO raises a question he's never faced in his career: with AI making the future genuinely invisible, should multiples be LOWER across the board — not just for software, but even for Home Depot or John Deere — a de-rating driven by tech-uncertainty rather than macro, and against an otherwise favorable macro backdrop that historically meant HIGHER multiples.
insights.speaker: Liberty Mutual Investments CIO
insights.so_what: A structural argument for compressed multiples from AI unpredictability — orthogonal to the usual rates/inflation framework.
insights.quote: should multiples be actually lower across the board?

insights.claim: He expects structurally higher volatility: 'you will likely have trillion-dollar companies in 2030 that currently don't exist' alongside trillion/hundred-billion-dollar companies that won't survive — compounded by potential moves like ending SEC quarterly reporting.
insights.speaker: Liberty Mutual Investments CIO
insights.so_what: Faster creative destruction at the mega-cap level implies persistently higher index/stock volatility — a regime shift to price.
insights.quote: you will likely have trillion-dollar companies in 2030 that currently don't exist

insights.claim: In credit, he's relaxed about 4-year software paper (contracted, money-good) but views 30-year credit on names like Salesforce or Oracle as much riskier — which should STEEPEN credit curves and change capital-market behavior.
insights.speaker: Liberty Mutual Investments CIO
insights.so_what: A concrete trade implication: long-dated incumbent-software credit is mispriced for AI disruption risk — favor curve steepeners.
insights.quote: that should drive steepness in credit curves

insights.claim: On Salesforce: even if every Fortune 500 uses it forever, the real valuation question is whether the not-yet-born trillion-dollar companies of 2030 will adopt it — if not, that's a massive headwind, turning it into a lower-multiple cash-cow business despite a sticky installed base.
insights.speaker: Liberty Mutual Investments CIO
insights.so_what: Reframes incumbent-software valuation around FUTURE-customer adoption, not current penetration — a lens for the whole SaaS complex.

insights.claim: The CIO says public markets are 'substantially more difficult to hold' than private (mark-to-market psychology) and that private-market growth is structural — the capital-need and prestige reasons to IPO are gone while public short-termism is costly — so the firm keeps its equity exposure largely private; notably, the 3-4 biggest private companies going public would be 3 of the 10 biggest public companies, unprecedented.
insights.speaker: Liberty Mutual Investments CIO
insights.so_what: Structural tailwind for private markets persists (capital availability), even if regulatory burdens mean-revert — shapes allocation.
insights.quote: public markets are substantially more difficult to hold than private markets

insights.claim: On AI in the investing process: it's a 'superpower' but demands engagement and agency — taking the first output yields 'slop' and averages, so you must inject your own knowledge/creativity and act as editor; he uses it daily but worries it's isolating (less time with colleagues).
insights.speaker: Liberty Mutual Investments CIO
insights.so_what: AI lifts the analyst who interrogates it and flattens the one who accepts first drafts — a usage discipline, plus a culture/isolation risk.
insights.quote: that's where slop tends to live

insights.claim: Managing your own balance sheet removes the 'business strategy dwarfs investment process' problem of third-party funds (fundraising cycles, investor updates, multiple-maximization) — but permanence risks complacency, with 'long-term' becoming an excuse; he counters by holding both truths ('the 10-year rate is just a series of shorter rates'), putting people on the hook for 3-5 year targets, and insisting 'transparency is what allows you to have autonomy.'
insights.speaker: Liberty Mutual Investments CIO
insights.so_what: A candid two-sided view of permanent capital — and a governance recipe (3-5yr accountability + transparency) to avoid its complacency trap.
insights.quote: transparency is what allows you to have autonomy. No transparency, no autonomy
